Um but I know that Kim had looked at it with you initially and then I just got really swamped.
So, yeah, thank you for your patience with me.
Yeah, of course.
Yeah, I think I had talked to Lindsay.
Um And so we got a little bit of a sense of what your kind of current process is for kind of evaluating the, the market your comps.
But, um, wanted to, to hear from you today and kind of hear more about what, what your goals are here in 2024 and, and what that, that process currently looks like.

Um, so I thought we could kind of start there if you don't mind and just so we get a good understanding of where things are at now and we can kind of try to figure out if, if I you might be able to help in, in some areas.
So, yeah, I mean, I think for our comp studies and our comp information, our managers are required to work with their on site teams, whether it be leasing or assistant manager.
And it is a pretty manual process right now, which has worked well for us through the years.

Not, I'm not saying whether it's still working well for us or not, but um as of today, part of the process or part of the reasoning for that process is forcing them to go through the motions and know more about their comps through a bit of a manual process.
So yes, they do call, yes, I'm aware that they probably don't always get 100% accurate information.

Um I think there's some valuable things that come from that and probably some, not some valuable things, one just being a relationship that they create with their comps by talking to their comps, whether it be in person or calling.
Um, and then again, just them having to go through that process at least tells me that.
Um, I mean, of course, unless they just lie and make up numbers, which they could do, um, if that's the case, we got the wrong people there to start with.
But, um, I feel pretty confident that they are making that, that contact with the comp every month And when I say comp, we're talking about four or five of their select comps.
Um So again, the, the second part of that process I think is valuable is them just, you know, ha being forced to, to know what their comps are doing.
Um So that's the valuable piece, obviously, the negatives are, you know, we don't know if that, how accurate the information is, whether the comps will share it with us.
Um You know, I'm sure in some markets it may not be as accurate.
Um Tulsa for instance, is not a big city.
I mean, it is, but it isn't, I mean, we're like a half million people.
So it's not, you know, you, you know, everybody in in the market pretty much.
Um So that works out pretty well for us, I think um Norman in Oklahoma City, which we have another big presence in is probably a little bit tougher to get accurate information.
And then Northwest Arkansas, we we're still fairly new to that market and nobody knows us, so I'm not sure that's accurate either.
Um, it's worked for us through the years.
I'm not sure whether it will continue to work for us moving forward being that things have changed so much, but that's what we're doing today.
Ok.
Got you.
And, um, are they doing that monthly typically?
Yes.

We pretty much do that manually too.

And then of course, just, you know, they're expected to call or visit the site in person uh or over the phone and then, you know, if they're looking to change rates according to what they get from the comps and, you know, they'll, they'll take it a step further and go online and look at their advertising.

What's their specials that they're showing?

And so I do have some scattered specials out there and they're really, I mean, they're all over the place there.

It could be a variety of reasons.

It makes me feel great.

vacant units is.
